Access them by:

title: EMRandlesLayer > EMRandlesLayer > EMRandlesLayer
text:
class ansys.dyna.core.pre.dynaem.EMRandlesLayer(*args, **kwds)

Bases: ansys.dyna.core.pre.dynabase.Enum

Create a collection of name/value pairs.

Example enumeration:

>>> class Color(Enum):
...     RED = 1
...     BLUE = 2
...     GREEN = 3

Access them by:

attribute access:

>>> Color.RED
<Color.RED: 1>

value lookup:

>>> Color(1)
<Color.RED: 1>

name lookup:

>>> Color['RED']
<Color.RED: 1>

Enumerations can be iterated over, and know how many members they have:

>>> len(Color)
3

>>> list(Color)
[<Color.RED: 1>, <Color.BLUE: 2>, <Color.GREEN: 3>]

Methods can be added to enumerations, and members can have their own
attributes – see the documentation for details.

!! processed by numpydoc !!

title: ICFDAnalysis > Method detail
text:
Set the type of the CFD analysis.
Analysis type. The default is TRANSIENT_ANALYSIS.
Modify default values for screen and file outputs related to the fluid solver only.
Message level. The default is TIMESTEP_INFORMATION.
Iteration interval to print the output at. The default is 0.
Modify default values for the fluid-structure interaction coupling algorithm.
Coupling direction to the solver. The default is TWO_WAY_COUPLING.
Set convergence options for the steady state solver.
Maximum number of iterations to reach convergence. The default is
1000000.0.
Tolerance limits for the momentum equations. The default is 0.001.
Tolerance limits for the pressure equations. The default is 0.001.
Tolerance limits for the temperature equations. The default is 0.001.
Relaxation parameters for the velocity.  The default is 0.3.
Relaxation parameters for the pressure.  The default is 0.7.
Set the time step for the fluid problem.
Time step for the fluid problem. The default is 0.
Modify the default value for automatic volume mesh generation.
Maximum mesh size that the volume mesher is allowed to use when generating
the volume mesh. The default is 1.41.
Activate the adaptive mesh refinement feature.
Minimum mesh size for the mesh generator. The default is 0.
Maximum mesh size. The default is 0.
Maximum perceptual error allowed in the whole domain. The default
is 0.
Number of iterations before a forced remeshing. The default is
0.
Enable automatic surface remeshing.
Whether to perform a surface remeshing. The default is LAPLACIAN_SMOOTHING.
Activate coupling between the ICFD and DEM solvers.
Coupling direction to the solver. The default is 0.
Birth time for the DEM coupling. The default is 0.
Death time for the DEM coupling.  The default is 1e+28.
Scale factor applied to the force transmitted by the fluid to
the structure. The default is 1.
Type of formulation to use in the coupling. The default is
FORCE_BASED_ON_VELOCITY_DRAG_VALUE.
Create ICFD analysis.

Adapter for keeping the reference to an MAPDL instance name dynamic.
Using the standard approach, extra parameters must be supplied
to the logger to indicate the MAPDL instance for which messages

title: AnalysisType > AnalysisType > AnalysisType
text:
class ansys.dyna.core.pre.dynabase.AnalysisType(*args, **kwds)

Bases: enum.Enum

Create a collection of name/value pairs.

Example enumeration:

>>> class Color(Enum):
...     RED = 1
...     BLUE = 2
...     GREEN = 3

Access them by:

attribute access:

>>> Color.RED
<Color.RED: 1>

value lookup:

>>> Color(1)
<Color.RED: 1>

name lookup:

>>> Color['RED']
<Color.RED: 1>

Enumerations can be iterated over, and know how many members they have:

>>> len(Color)
3

>>> list(Color)
[<Color.RED: 1>, <Color.BLUE: 2>, <Color.GREEN: 3>]

Methods can be added to enumerations, and members can have their own
attributes – see the documentation for details.

Execution stops until told to resume or quit.  Most switch”
commands result in one cycle being executed so that the switch can
be handled, and then LS-DYNA pauses again.

working directory of the calling process. The file name should not

with any path components removed.

command and continues running.

stdout is used. Otherwise, stderr is used.
How to handle the file data. The default is 2. Options are:
0: The file contents are printed to stdout, and this
method does not return until LS-DYNA terminates.
1: A Queue instance is returned. The queue holds raw blocks
of text as returned by the server.

title: DynaSolver > Method detail > pause
text:
DynaSolver.pause()

Pause LS-DYNA execution.

Execution stops until told to resume or quit.  Most switch”
commands result in one cycle being executed so that the switch can
be handled, and then LS-DYNA pauses again.

title: DynaSolver > Method detail > quit

If the server is running inside a container, it ignore this

title: DynaSolver > Method detail > resume
text:
DynaSolver.resume(cycle=None, time=None)

Resume execution.

Parameters

cycle

integer, optional

Cycle to pause execution on. The default is None.

time

python:float, optional

Ssimulation time to pause execution at. The default is None.

This command can be given whether LS-DYNA is paused or running.



LS-DYNA runs until the given cycle number or simulation time. If



both are given, it stops based on whichever occurs first. If



neither are given, it runs until termination or until paused.



!! processed by numpydoc !!

title: DynaSolver > Method detail > tail
text:
DynaSolver.tail(which=1, how=2, queuesize=0)

Monitor the stdout or stderr of the running job.

Parameters

which

python:int, optional

Which stream to monitor. The default is 1, in which case
stdout is used. Otherwise, stderr is used.

how

python:int, optional

How to handle the file data. The default is 2. Options are:

0: The file contents are printed to stdout, and this
method does not return until LS-DYNA terminates.

1: A Queue instance is returned. The queue holds raw blocks
of text as returned by the server.

2: A Queue instance is returned.The queue holds individual
lines of text.

queuesize

python:int, optional

Size to use for the Queue instance if the how parameter is set to
a value greater than 0.

Returns

If the value for the how parameter is greater than 0, a Queue instance



is returned. When a call to the q.get() method returns None, all data



has been returned.



!! processed by numpydoc !!

Returns

title: DynaSolver > Method detail > send
text:
DynaSolver.send(cmdin)

Send one request to LS-DYNA using the command-line interface.

Parameters

cmdin

python:str

Command to send.

Returns

python:None

There is no data returned. Data that is returned from the underlying
method call is just printed to the screen. This method is used to
support the sample interactive client.py program.





Description

Example

“list” then optional file name

list d3plot

“list” then optional file name

list d3plot

“node” user node number

node 43444

“pause” (no arguments)

pause

“download” then file name

download glstat

“upload” then file name

upload input.k

“quit” (no arguments)

quit

“resume” optional cycle and/or time

resume 5000 0.25

“run” then the command line

run i=input.k jobid=xx

“setlc” user load curve number and value

setlc 1075 0.245

“start” number of processors

start 5

“switch” then switch text

switch sw2

“tail” then 1 for stdout, 2 for stderr

tail 1

“time” (no arguments)

time.

!! processed by numpydoc !!

title: DynaISPH > Method detail
text:
Provide controls related to ISPH.
Number of time steps between particle sorting. The default
is 1.
Box ID. The default is 0. ISPH approximations are computed
inside the specified box. When a particle has gone outside this
box, it is deactivated.
Space dimension for ISPH particles. The default is 3. Options
are:
EQ.3: 3D problems
EQ.2: 2D plane strain problems
EQ.-2: 2D axisymmetric problems
Initial number of neighbors per particle. The default is 150.
Particle approximation theory. The default is 0.
Maximum value for velocity for the ISPH particles. The default
is 1000000000000000.0.
True when successful, False when failed.
Save keyword files.
True when successful, False when failed.

title: DynaISPH > Method detail > set_des
text:
DynaISPH.set_des(num_timestep=1, boxid=0, space_dimension=3, neighbors=150, approximation_theory=0, max_velocty=1000000000000000.0)

Provide controls related to ISPH.

Parameters

num_timestep

python:int, optional

Number of time steps between particle sorting. The default
is 1.

boxid

python:int, optional

Box ID. The default is 0. ISPH approximations are computed
inside the specified box. When a particle has gone outside this
box, it is deactivated.

space_dimension

python:int, optional

Space dimension for ISPH particles. The default is 3. Options
are:

EQ.3: 3D problems

EQ.2: 2D plane strain problems

EQ.-2: 2D axisymmetric problems

neighbors

python:int, optional

Initial number of neighbors per particle. The default is 150.

approximation_theory

python:int, optional

Particle approximation theory. The default is 0.

max_velocty

python:float, optional

Maximum value for velocity for the ISPH particles. The default
is 1000000000000000.0.

Returns

bool

True when successful, False when failed.

Create a collection of name/value pairs.

Example enumeration:

>>> class Color(Enum):
...     RED = 1
...     BLUE = 2
...     GREEN = 3

Access them by:

attribute access:

>>> Color.RED
<Color.RED: 1>

value lookup:

>>> Color(1)
<Color.RED: 1>

name lookup:

>>> Color['RED']
<Color.RED: 1>

Enumerations can be iterated over, and know how many members they have:

>>> len(Color)
3

>>> list(Color)
[<Color.RED: 1>, <Color.BLUE: 2>, <Color.GREEN: 3>]

Methods can be added to enumerations, and members can have their own
attributes – see the documentation for details.

text:
Set the mortar contact.
A mort contact is a segment-to-segment, penalty-based contact.
Set the contact allow for failure.
A tieback is a special case of this. After failure, the contact
usually becomes a normal one-way, two-way, or single surface version.
Set the coefficient of friction.
Static coefficient of friction. The default is 0.
Dynamic coefficient of friction.  The default is 0.
Set the birth and death time to active and deactivate the contact.
Time to activate the contact. The default is 0.

Ignore initial penetrations.

class ansys.dyna.core.pre.dynabase.InvariantNode(*args, **kwds)

Bases: enum.Enum

Create a collection of name/value pairs.

Example enumeration:

>>> class Color(Enum):
...     RED = 1
...     BLUE = 2
...     GREEN = 3

Access them by:

attribute access:

>>> Color.RED
<Color.RED: 1>

value lookup:

>>> Color(1)
<Color.RED: 1>

name lookup:

>>> Color['RED']
<Color.RED: 1>

Enumerations can be iterated over, and know how many members they have:

>>> len(Color)
3

>>> list(Color)
[<Color.RED: 1>, <Color.BLUE: 2>, <Color.GREEN: 3>]

Methods can be added to enumerations, and members can have their own
attributes – see the documentation for details.

title: Getting started > Installation
text:
PyDYNA consists of four modules, ansys.dyna.core.pre, ansys.dyna.core.solver,
ansys.dyna.core.run, and ansys.dyna.core.keywords.
ansys.dyna.core.pre and ansys.dyna.core.solver use gRPC and hence need to be run
using server-client connection.
Installation
ansys.dyna.core.pre
ansys.dyna.core.solver
ansys.dyna.core.run
ansys.dyna.core.keywords
ansys.dyna.core.pre
ansys.dyna.core.solver

title: Getting started > Installation > Install the client
text:
The ansys.dyna.core package supports Python 3.9 through
Python 3.12 on Windows, Linux, and MacOS.
You should consider installing PyDYNA in a virtual environment.
For more information, see Python’s
venv – Creation of virtual environments.
PyDYNA has three installation modes: user, developer, and offline.
Install the client
ansys.dyna.core

title: Getting started > Installation > Install in user mode
text:
Before installing PyDYNA in user mode, make sure you have the latest version of
pip with this command:
Then, install PyDYNA with this command:
Install in user mode

title: Getting started > Installation > Install in developer mode
text:
Installing PyDYNA in developer mode allows you to modify the source and enhance it.
Before contributing to the project, ensure that you are thoroughly familiar
with the PyAnsys Developer’s Guide.
Start by cloning and installing the repository with these commands:
Install in developer mode

title: Getting started > Installation > Install in offline mode
text:
If you lack an internet connection on your installation machine, you should install
PyDYNA by downloading the wheelhouse archive for your corresponding machine
architecture from the Releases Page.
Each wheelhouse archive contains all the Python wheels necessary to install
PyDYNA from scratch on Windows and Linux for Python 3.9 through 3.12. You can install
PyDYNA on an isolated system with a fresh Python installation or on a virtual environment.
For example, on Linux with Python 3.9, unzip the wheelhouse archive and install PyDYNA
with these commands:
If you’re on Windows with Python 3.9, unzip the wheelhouse archive to a wheelhouse
directory and install PyDYNA using the preceding command.
Install in offline mode
wheelhouse

from the args and kwargs arguments, respectively.

title: DefineFunction > Property detail
text:
Get or set the Function ID.
Functions, tables (see *DEFINE_TABLE), and load curves may not share common ID’s.
A unique number has to be defined.
Get or set the An optional descriptive heading.
Get or set the Arithmetic expression involving a combination
of independent variables and other functions.
,i.e., f(a,b,c)=a*2+b*c+sqrt(a*c)
where a, b, and c are the independent variables.  The function name, f(a,b,c), must be
unique since other functions can then use and reference this function.
For example, g(a,b,c,d)=f(a,b,c)**2+d.  In this example, two *DEFINE_ FUNCTION definitions
are needed to define functions f and g.
Property detail

This example shows how to create an airbag deploy model with the PyDNYA pre service.
The executable file for LS-DYNA is ls-dyna_smp_d_R13.0_365-gf8a97bda2a_winx64_ifort190.exe.

title: Airbag deploy > Start the pre service
text:
Before starting the pre service, you must ensure that the Docker container
for this service has been started. For more information, see “Start the Docker
container for the pre service” in https://dyna.docs.pyansys.com/version/stable/index.html.
The pre service can also be started locally, please download the latest version of
ansys-pydyna-pre-server.zip package from https://github.com/ansys/pydyna/releases and start it
referring to the README.rst file in this server package.
Once the pre service is running, you can connect a client to it using
the hostname and port. This example uses the default localhost and port
("localhost" and "50051" respectively).
Start the pre service

title: Airbag deploy > Create standard explicit control cards
text:
The following code uses the set_termination method to set the termination time
to 0.03 in CONTROL_TERMINATION. The DynaMech class
automatically generates the common control cards used in
explicit problems. CONTROL_ACCURACY, CONTACT, BULK VISCOCITY,
and CONTACT are all automatically generated.

title: Airbag deploy > Define a keyword
text:
Use the Airbag function in the DynaMech class to define
AIRBAG_SIMPLE_AIRBAG_MODEL as a keyword. While LS-DYNA has many different
airbag models, PyDYNA currently supports only one: SIMPLE_AIRBAG_MODEL.
If you have an urgent need for PyDYNA to support another airbag model, email
pyansys.core@ansys.com with your request.

title: Airbag deploy > Define material cards
text:
LS-DYNA has over 300 materials that are used for varied applications.
While PyDYNA does not yet support all material cards, it does support
most commonly used materials, including FABRIC, MAT_ELASTIC,
PIECEWISE_LINEAR_PLASTICITY, and RIGID. All supported materials
are accessed from the dynamaterial class. In the following code,
MAT_RIGID is defined as the material  for the cylindrical tube and the
bottom plate. MAT_FABRIC is defined as the material for the airbag volume.
Note that platemat also has constraints defined.
Define material cards
FABRIC

dynamaterial

title: Airbag deploy > Define sectional properties
text:
The following code defines the sectional properties of the parts. This example
has three shell parts. Each shell part is initialized as ShellPart with a
unique ID and an appropriate shell formulation is assigned. Again,
PyDYNA does not yet support all element formulations. You can find the
supported formulations in the dynabase class.
Define sectional properties
ShellPart
dynabase

title: Airbag deploy > Define database outputs
text:
Use the set_output_database() and create_database_binary() methods to define the
output frequency of the ASCII and binary D3PLOT files. Then, use the save_file()
method to write out the model as an input DYNA key file.

title: The array.py module > Summary
text:
array
A resizable array that supports optional values for any type
Summary
array

title: The array.py module > Module detail
text:
A resizable array that supports optional values for any type
Right now - array.array is used for single and double precision floating points
for everything else - python list is used.  This is because no existing array
type in numpy, pandas, and python meet the above requirements.  Specifically,
numpy integer arrays do not have optional values and are not resizable, pandas
integer arrays support optional values but are also not resizable, while python
array arrays are resizable but do not support optional values.
The problem with this approach is memory usage.  For 100k integers, a python list
appears to take about 5300K, while a pandas array and numpy array take 488K and 584K
respectively.  pandas arrays take more memory than numpy because of the masking used
to support optional integer values.
Given a python list of optional integer, where None is used to represent a missing value,
- this is how you convert to either type:
numpy:  np.array([item or 0 for item in the_list], dtype=np.int32)
pandas: pd.array(the_list,dtype=pd.Int32Dtype())
In the future - A dynamic array class based on some of the above types can be used for
integer arrays.  For string arrays, pandas arrays don’t offer any value over python
lists.
Module detail

text:

A resizable array that supports optional values for any type
Right now - array.array is used for single and double precision floating points
for everything else - python list is used.  This is because no existing array
type in numpy, pandas, and python meet the above requirements.  Specifically,
numpy integer arrays do not have optional values and are not resizable, pandas
integer arrays support optional values but are also not resizable, while python
array arrays are resizable but do not support optional values.

The problem with this approach is memory usage.  For 100k integers, a python list
appears to take about 5300K, while a pandas array and numpy array take 488K and 584K
respectively.  pandas arrays take more memory than numpy because of the masking used
to support optional integer values.

Given a python list of optional integer, where None is used to represent a missing value,
- this is how you convert to either type:
numpy:  np.array([item or 0 for item in the_list], dtype=np.int32)
pandas: pd.array(the_list,dtype=pd.Int32Dtype())

In the future - A dynamic array class based on some of the above types can be used for
integer arrays.  For string arrays, pandas arrays don’t offer any value over python
lists.

title: Contribute
text:
Overall guidance on contributing to a PyAnsys library appears in the
Contributing topic
in the PyAnsys Developer’s Guide. Ensure that you are thoroughly familiar
with this guide before attempting to contribute to PyDYNA.
The following contribution information is specific to PyDYNA.
Contribute

title: Contribute > Clone the repository
text:
To clone and install the latest PyDYNA release in development mode, run these commands:
Clone the repository

title: Contribute > Post issues
text:
Use the PyDYNA Issues
page to report bugs and request new features. When possible,
use these issue templates:
Bug, problem, error: For filing a bug report
Documentation issue: For requesting modifications to the documentation
Adding an example: For proposing a new example
New feature: For requesting enhancements to the code
If your issue does not fit into one of these template categories, you can click
the link for opening a blank issue.
To reach the project support team, email pyansys.core@ansys.com.
Post issues

title: Contribute > View documentation
text:
Documentation for the latest stable release of PyDYNA is hosted at
PyDYNA documentation.
In the upper right corner of the documentation’s title bar, there is an option
for switching from viewing the documentation for the latest stable release to
viewing the documentation for the development version or previously released versions.
View documentation

title: Contribute > Adhere to code style
text:
PyDYNA follows the PEP8 standard as outlined in the PyAnsys Developer’s Guide and implements style checking using
pre-commit.
To ensure your code meets minimum code styling standards, run these commands:
You can also install this as a pre-commit hook by running this command:
This way, it is not possible for you to push code that fails the style checks:
Adhere to code style

title: FRF for a rectangular plate
text:
This example shows how to set up the keywords for computation of a FRF (frequency response function).
The executable file for LS-DYNA is ls-dyna_smp_d_R13.1_138-g8429c8a10f_winx64_ifort190.exe.
FRF for a rectangular plate
ls-dyna_smp_d_R13.1_138-g8429c8a10f_winx64_ifort190.exe

title: FRF for a rectangular plate > Perform required imports
text:
Perform the required imports.
Perform required imports

title: FRF for a rectangular plate > Start the pre service
text:
Before starting the pre service, you must ensure that the Docker container
for this service has been started. For more information, see “Start the Docker
container for the pre service” in https://dyna.docs.pyansys.com/version/stable/index.html.
The pre service can also be started locally, please download the latest version of
ansys-pydyna-pre-server.zip package from https://github.com/ansys/pydyna/releases and start it
referring to the README.rst file in this server package.
Once the pre service is running, you can connect a client to it using
the hostname and port. This example uses the default localhost and port
("localhost" and "50051" respectively).
Start the pre service
pre
pre
pre
pre
pre
"localhost"
"50051"

title: FRF for a rectangular plate > Import mesh data (nodes and elements)
text:
Import initial mesh data, which includes the predefined NODE, ELEMENT_ and PART
cards.
Import mesh data (nodes and elements)

title: FRF for a rectangular plate > Define global control cards
text:
Define the global control cards.
Define global control cards

title: FRF for a rectangular plate > Set initial timestep size
text:
Set the initial timestep size in CONTROL_IMPLICIT_GENERAL.
Set initial timestep size

title: FRF for a rectangular plate > Set number of eigen modes
text:
Set the number of eigen modes to 100.
Set number of eigen modes
100

title: FRF for a rectangular plate > Define linear solver
text:
Define the linear solver by setting NSOLVR to 1 in CONTROL_IMPLICIT_SOLUTION.
Define linear solver
1

title: FRF for a rectangular plate > Define frequency domain cards
text:
FREQUENCY_DOMAIN_FRF is used to compute the frequency response function due to
nodal excitations. In this case, a base velocity is defined as an input at node 131.
The base acceleration response is measured at nodes 131 and 651. The maximum
natural frequency employed in FRF is limited to 2000 Hz.
Define frequency domain cards

title: FRF for a rectangular plate > Define material and section properties
text:
Define the MAT_ELASTIC material. Set shell formulation to SR_HUGHES_LIU.
Define material and section properties
MAT_ELASTIC
SR_HUGHES_LIU

Set the structural time step size control using different options.

python:float, optional

Scale factor for computed time step. The default is 0.9.

isdo

python:int, optional

Basis of the time size calculation for four-node shell elements.
The default is 0.

python:float, optional

Time step size for mass scaled solutions. The default is 0.0.

Curve, optional

Load curve that limits the maximum time step size. The default
is None.

bool

True when successful, False when failed.

title: DynaBase > Method detail > set_output
text:
DynaBase.set_output(print_suppression_d3hsp=False, print_suppression_echo=OutputEcho.ALL_DATA_PRINTED)

Set miscellaneous output parameters.

Parameters

print_suppression_d3hsp

bool, optional

Whether to suppress printing during the input phase flag for the D3HSP file.
The default is True, which means that none of these are printed: nodal
coordinates, element connectivities, rigid wall definitions, nodal SPCs,
initial velocities, initial strains, adaptive constraints, and SPR2/SPR3
constraints. If False, no suppression occurs.

print_suppression_echo

OutputEcho

Print suppression setting during the input phase flag for the echo file.
Options are:

ALL_DATA_PRINTED: All data is printed.

SUPPRESSED_NODAL_PRINTING: Nodal printing is suppressed.

SUPPRESSED_ELEMENT_PRINTING: Element printing is suppressed.

SUPPRESSED_NODAL_AND_ELEMENT_PRINTING : Both nodal and element printing is suppressed.

title: DynaBase > Method detail > create_control_shell
text:
DynaBase.create_control_shell(wrpang=20, esort=0, irnxx=-1, istupd=0, theory=2, bwc=2, miter=1, proj=0, irquad=0)

Provide controls for computing shell response.

Parameters

wrpang

python:int, optional

Shell element warpage angle in degrees. The default is 20.

esort

python:int

Sorting of triangular shell elements to automatically switch
degenerate quadrilateral shell formulations to more suitable
triangular shell formulations. The default is 0.

irnxx

python:int, optional

Shell normal update option. The default is 1.

istupd

python:int, optional

Shell thickness change option for deformable shells. The
default is 0.

theory

python:int, optional

Default shell formulation. The default is 2.

bwc

python:int, optional

Warping stiffness for Belytschko-Tsay shells. The
default is 2.

miter

python:int, optional

Plane stress plasticity option. The default is 1.

proj

python:int, optional

Projection method for the warping stiffness in the Belytschko-Tsay
shell and the Belytschko-Wong-Chiang elements. The default is
0.

irquad

python:int, optional

In-plane integration rule for the eight-node quadratic shell element.
The default is 0.

EQ.2: 2*2 Gauss quadrature

EQ.3: 3*3 Gauss quadrature

Returns

bool

True when successful, False when failed.

!! processed by numpydoc !!

title: DynaBase > Method detail > create_control_solid
text:
DynaBase.create_control_solid(esort=0, fmatrx=0, niptets=4, swlocl=1, psfail=0, t10jtol=0.0, icoh=0, tet13k=0)

Provide controls for a solid element response.

Parameters

esort

python:int, optional

Automatic sorting of tetrahedral and pentahedral elements to avoid
use of degenerate formulations for these shapes. The default is 0.

EQ.0: No sorting

EQ.1: Sort

fmatrx

python:int, optional

Method to use in the calculation of the deformation gradient matrix.
The default is 1.

niptets

python:int, optional

Number of integration points used in the quadratic tetrahedron elements.
The default is 4.

swlocl

python:int, optional

Output option for stresses in solid elements used as spot welds with
material \*MAT_SPOTWELD. The default is 1.

psfail

python:int, optional

Solid element erosion from negative volume is limited only to solid elements in
the part set indicated by PSFAIL. The default is 0.

t10jtol

python:float, optional

Tolerance for Jacobian in four-point, 10-noded quadratic tetrahedra. The
default is 0.0.

icoh

python:int, optional

Breaking LS-DYNA convention ICOH is interpreted digit-wise. The default
is 0.

tet13k

python:int, optional

Flag for whether to invoke a consistent tangent stiffness matrix
for the pressure averaged tetrahedron. The default is 0, in which
case this matrix is not invoked. If this parameter is set to 1,
this matrix is invoked.

Returns

bool

True when successful, False when failed.

!! processed by numpydoc !!

title: DynaBase > Method detail > create_control_contact
text:
DynaBase.create_control_contact(rwpnal, shlthk=0, orien=1, ssthk=0, ignore=0, igactc=0)

Change defaults for computation with contact surfaces.

Parameters

rwpnal

python:float

Scale factor for rigid wall penalties, which treat nodal points interacting
with rigid walls.

shlthk

python:int, optional

Flag for whether to consider shell thickness offsets in non-automatic
surface-to-surface and non-automatic nodes-to-surface type contacts.
The default is 0, in which case these offsets are not considered.
If this parameter is set to 1, these offsets are considered.

orien

python:int, optional

Flag for whether to automatically reorient contact interface segments
during initialization. The default is 1, in which case reorientation
automatically occurs. If this parameter is set to 0, reorientation
does not occur.

ssthk

python:int, optional

Flag for whether to determine default contact thickness for shells in single
surface contact types. The default is 0, in which case default contact
thickness is not determined. If this parameter is set to 1, default
contact thickness is determined.

ignore

python:int, optional

Flag for whether to ignore initial penetrations in the \*CONTACT_AUTOMATIC
options. The default is 0, in which case initial penetrations are ignored.
If this parameter is set to 1, initial penetrations are not ignored.

igactc

python:int, optional

Flag for whether to use isogeometric shells for contact detection when the
contact involves isogeometric shells. The default is 0, which means
isogeometric shells are not used. If this parameter is set to 1, isogeometric
shells are used.

Returns

bool

True when successful, False when failed.

!! processed by numpydoc !!

title: DynaBase > Method detail > create_damping_global
text:
DynaBase.create_damping_global(lcid=0, valdmp=0.0)

Define mass-weighted nodal damping.

Mass-weighted nodal damping applies globally to the
nodes of deformable bodies and to the mass center of
rigid bodies.

Parameters

lcid

python:int, optional

Load curve ID, which specifies the system damping constant
versus the time. The default is 0.

valdmp

python:float, optional

System damping constant. The default is 0.0.

Returns

bool

True when successful, False when failed.

!! processed by numpydoc !!

title: DynaBase > Method detail > create_general_keyword
text:
DynaBase.create_general_keyword(opcode, keyworddata)

Create general keyword.

Parameters

opcode

python:str

Keyword card name.

keyworddata

string

Keyword data.

Example::

Create a \*INITIAL_VELOCITY keyword.

$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$
*INITIAL_VELOCITY

&       vx        vy        vz       vxr       vyr       vzr
1.480E+01 0.000E+00 0.000E+00 0.000E+00 0.000E+00 0.000E+00
$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$$

opcode = “INITIAL_VELOCITY”
keyworddata = “0

1.480E+01,0.000E+00,0.000E+00,0.000E+00,0.000E+00,0.000E+00”

create_general_keyword(opcode = opcode,keyworddata=keyworddata)

title: Getting started > Server > Build the Docker image for the pre service
section: Build the Docker image for the pre service
text:
You must build the Docker image for the PyDYNA pre service and then
run the image as a container.
Build the Docker image for the pre service
pre
pre

title: Getting started > Server > Prerequisites
text:
Ensure that you have cloned the PyDYNA repository locally with these commands:
The docker file in the  docker/pre directory is used to build the
Linux-based Docker image.
If you are building the image on Windows, ensure that the Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL)
is installed. For installation information, see Microsoft’s
Install Linux on Windows with WSL.
Install docker engine. Based on the Linux distro you can use the corresponding installation
instructions from this page.
Download the latest Linux release artifacts for the pre Docker container:
linux-binaries.zip.
Move this ZIP file to the docker/pre directory.
Once all prerequisites are met, you can build the Docker container for the pre service.
Prerequisites

In your terminal, go to the docker directory.
Run the following Docker command, replacing <DOCKERFILE_NAME>
with Dockerfile and <DOCKER_IMAGE_TAG> with latest.
Check that the image has been built successfully by running this command:
Your output should look similar to this:

title: Getting started > Server > Prerequisites
text:
Ensure that you have cloned the PyDYNA repository locally with these commands:
If you are building the image on Windows, ensure that the Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL)
is installed. For installation information, see Microsoft’s
Install Linux on Windows with WSL.
Install docker engine. Based on the Linux distro you can use the corresponding installation
instructions from this page.
Download the latest Linux release artifacts for the solver Docker container:
mppdyna_docker_centos7.zip.
Move this ZIP file to the docker/solver directory.
The files in this folder should look similar to this:
Once all prerequisites are met, you can build the Docker image for the solver service.

title: The launcher.py module > Module detail > port_in_use
text:
launcher.port_in_use(port, host=LOCALHOST)

Determine if a port is in use at a given host.

Parameters

port

python:int

Port.

host

Host. The default is LOCALHOST, in which case "127.0.0.1"

is used.

Returns

True when a port is in use at the given host, False otherwise.



Notes

The port must “bind” the address. Just checking if a socket can be created
is insufficient because it is possible to run into permission
errors like this one:

“An attempt was made to access a socket in a way forbidden by its
access permissions.”

title: The launcher.py module > Module detail > launch_remote_dyna
text:
launcher.launch_remote_dyna(version=None, cleanup_on_exit=True) -> ansys.dyna.core.solver.DynaSolver

Start DYNA remotely using the product instance management API.

When calling this method, you need to ensure that you are in an environment where PyPIM is configured.
This can be verified with pypim.is_configured.

Parameters

version

python:str, optional

The DYNA version to run, in the 3 digits format, such as “212”.

If unspecified, the version will be chosen by the server.

cleanup_on_exit

bool, optional

Exit DYNA when python exits or the dyna Python instance is
garbage collected.

If unspecified, it will be cleaned up.

Returns

ansys.dyna.core.solver.DynaSolver

An instance of DynaSolver.

!! processed by numpydoc !!

title: The launcher.py module > Module detail > launch_dyna
text:
launcher.launch_dyna(product_version: int = None, port=None, ip=None) -> ansys.dyna.core.solver.DynaSolver

Start DYNA locally.

Parameters

product_version: int, optional

The product version to be started. Goes from v20.1 to
the latest. Default is None.
If a specific product version is requested but not installed locally,
a SystemError will be raised.

Ansys products versions and their corresponding int values:

241 : Ansys 24R1

242 : Ansys 24R2

port

python:int

Port to launch DYNA gRPC on.  Final port will be the first
port available after (or including) this port.  Defaults to
5000.

ip

bool, optional

You can provide a hostname as an alternative to an IP address.
Defaults to '127.0.0.1'.

Examples

Launch DYNA using the best protocol.

>>> from ansys.dyna.core.solver import launch_dyna
>>> solution = launch_dyna()

Connect to an existing instance of DYNA at IP 192.168.1.30 and
port 5000.

>>> solution = launch_dyna(ip='192.168.1.30',port=5000)

title: PyDYNA documentation  0.9.dev0
text:
Learn how to install and use PyDYNA, including how to set up the
environment and run the solver.
Understand how to use PyDYNA’s features and capabilities,.
Explore the API reference documentation for PyDYNA, including
detailed descriptions of classes, methods, and functions.
Browse a collection of examples that demonstrate how to use PyDYNA
for various applications.
Find out how to contribute to the PyDYNA project, including how to
report issues and submit code changes.
View the latest changes and updates to PyDYNA, including new features
and bug fixes.
PyDYNA documentation  0.9.dev0

title: User guide
text:
In the PyDYNA installation, the docker directory has three child
directories:
pre: Contains the package with the pre Docker image for the
pre service. This service provides highly abstracted APIs for creating and
setting up DYNA input decks for DynaMech, DynaIGA, DynaICFD, DynaSALE, DynaEM,
and DynaAirbag.
solver: Contains the package with the dynasolver Docker image
for the solver service. This service provides highly abstracted
APIs for interacting directly with the Ansys LS-DYNA solver. Because LS-DYNA
is primarily a batch solver with very limited interactive capabilities, the
solver service is similarly limited. The target use case is that LS-DYNA is
running in a container environment such as Docker or Kubernetes. Using this
service, you can push input files to the container, start LS-DYNA
and monitor its progress, and then retrieve Ansys solver results (RST)
files.
run: This module provides the ability to start the LS-DYNA solver. This does not require any
client-server library or Docker container.
Once you have results, you can use the Ansys Data Processing Framework (DPF),
which is designed to provide numerical simulation users and engineers
with a toolbox for accessing and transforming simulation data. DPF
can access data from Ansys solver RST files and from several
files with neutral formats, including CSV, HDF5, and VTK. Using DPF’s
various operators, you can manipulate and transform this data.
The ansys-dpf-post package provides
a simplified Python interface to DPF, thus enabling rapid postprocessing
without ever leaving a Python environment. For more information on DPF-Post,
see the DPF-Post documentation.
User guide

title: Mat295 > Method detail
text:
Abstract method to peek into the buffer before reading.
Normally, the number of rows is calculated by the card, and is based on whether
the card is active or not. In most cases, a card will know whether it is active
or not before it is read, because the active_func is based on information loaded
into previous cards. In some cases, active_func is based on information from the
current card, which might default to inactive, and so the card will never be read.
Method detail

title: Mat295 > Method detail > before_read
text:
Mat295.before_read(buf: TextIO) -> None

Abstract method to peek into the buffer before reading.

Normally, the number of rows is calculated by the card, and is based on whether
the card is active or not. In most cases, a card will know whether it is active
or not before it is read, because the active_func is based on information loaded
into previous cards. In some cases, active_func is based on information from the
current card, which might default to inactive, and so the card will never be read.

Set miscellaneous output parameters.
Print suppression during the input phase flag for the D3HSP file.
The default is 0. Options are:
EQ.0: No suppression.
EQ.1: Nodal coordinates, element connectivities, rigid wall definitions,
nodal SPCs, initial velocities, initial strains, adaptive constraints, and
SPR2/SPR3 constraints are not printed.
Print suppression during the input phase flag for the echo file.
The default is 0. Options are:
EQ.0: All data is printed.
EQ.1: Nodal printing is suppressed.
EQ.2: Element printing is suppressed.
EQ.3: Both nodal and element printing is suppressed.
True when successful, False when failed.
Define initial nodal point velocities using a nodal set ID.
Nodal set ID.
Initial translational velocity in the x,y,z-direction.
Initial rotational velocity about the x,y,z-axis.
True when successful, False when failed.
Define orientation vectors for discrete springs and dampers.
Orientation vector ID.
Options are:
EQ.0: Deflections/rotations are measured and forces/moments are applied
along the following orientation vector.
EQ.1: Deflections/rotations are measured and forces/moments are applied
along the axis between the two spring/damper nodes projected onto the plane normal
to the following orientation vector.
EQ.2: Deflections/rotations are measured and forces/moments are applied
along a vector defined by the following two nodes.
EQ.3: Deflections/rotations are measured and forces/moments are applied
along the axis between the two spring/damper nodes projected onto the
plane normal to the a vector defined by the following two nodes.
x,y,z : x,y,z-value of the orientation vector.

EQ.1: Material describes a torsional spring/damper.

title: DynaMech > Method detail > create_control_output
text:
DynaMech.create_control_output(npopt=0, neecho=0)

Set miscellaneous output parameters.

Parameters

npopt

python:int, optional

Print suppression during the input phase flag for the D3HSP file.
The default is 0. Options are:

EQ.0: No suppression.

EQ.1: Nodal coordinates, element connectivities, rigid wall definitions,
nodal SPCs, initial velocities, initial strains, adaptive constraints, and
SPR2/SPR3 constraints are not printed.

neecho

python:int, optional

Print suppression during the input phase flag for the echo file.
The default is 0. Options are:

EQ.0: All data is printed.

EQ.1: Nodal printing is suppressed.

EQ.2: Element printing is suppressed.

EQ.3: Both nodal and element printing is suppressed.

Returns

bool

True when successful, False when failed.

title: DynaMech > Method detail > create_defineorientation
text:
DynaMech.create_defineorientation(vid, iop, vector, node1, node2)

Define orientation vectors for discrete springs and dampers.

Parameters

vid

python:int

Orientation vector ID.

iop

python:int

Options are:

EQ.0: Deflections/rotations are measured and forces/moments are applied
along the following orientation vector.

EQ.1: Deflections/rotations are measured and forces/moments are applied
along the axis between the two spring/damper nodes projected onto the plane normal
to the following orientation vector.

EQ.2: Deflections/rotations are measured and forces/moments are applied
along a vector defined by the following two nodes.

EQ.3: Deflections/rotations are measured and forces/moments are applied
along the axis between the two spring/damper nodes projected onto the
plane normal to the a vector defined by the following two nodes.

vector

python:list [x,y,z]

x,y,z : x,y,z-value of the orientation vector.

node1

python:int

Node 1 ID.

node2

python:int

Node 2 ID.

Returns

bool

True when successful, False when failed.

Enumerations can be iterated over, and know how many members they have:

3

[<Color.RED: 1>, <Color.BLUE: 2>, <Color.GREEN: 3>]

This example shows how to use the PyDYNA pre service to create
a belted dummy model. The executable file for LS-DYNA is
ls-dyna_smp_d_R13.0_365-gf8a97bda2a_winx64_ifort190.exe.

title: Belted dummy > Start the pre service
text:
Before starting the pre service, you must ensure that the Docker container
for this service has been started. For more information, see “Start the Docker
container for the pre service” in https://dyna.docs.pyansys.com/version/stable/index.html.
The pre service can also be started locally, please download the latest version of
ansys-pydyna-pre-server.zip package from https://github.com/ansys/pydyna/releases and start it
referring to the README.rst file in this server package.
Once the pre service is running, you can connect a client to it using
the host name and port. This example uses the default localhost and port
("localhost" and "50051" respectively).

title: Belted dummy > Define materials
text:
In this model, many parts share common material types. Thus, these materials
are generated in a loop and a list of these materials are created. This list
can then be used later to assign materials to parts. The dynamaterials class
are used to define these materials: MAT_RIGID, MAT_ELASTIC,
MAT_SPRING_NONLINEAR_ELASTIC, MAT_DAMPER_VISCOUS, and
MAT_DAMPER_NONLINEAR_VISCOUS.
Define materials

title: Belted dummy > Define section properties and assign materials
section: Define section properties and assign materials
text:
Now that you have a list of materials with the material ID corresponding to
the part ID, you can loop through the list and assign these materials to the
parts. While in the loop, also define the section properties, element
formulations, and constraints.
Define section properties and assign materials

Run the Ls-Dyna solver with the given input file.

input

The mpi option to use. Choose from the values defined in MpiOption.
Defaults to MpiOption.SMP.

precision

Version of Ansys Unified installed to use.
Defaults to: TODO (find the latest one?).

Optional and Linux-Only: The name of the DYNA solver executable.
Default is s based on the value of the mpi_option argument.
On linux: it can be the full path.
Also on linux, ansys-tools-path can be used to save a custom location of
a dyna executable so that it doesn’t need to be set here each time.

ncpu

int

int

Working directory.
If the input parameter is a path to the input file,

in a new folder under $TMP/ansys/pydyna/jobs.

container

str

DockerContainer to run LS-DYNA in.

container_env

dict()

Environment variables to pass into the docker container.

stream

bool

Currently only affects runs using the container option.
If True, the stdout of solver is streamed to python’s stdout during the solve.
If False, the solver stdout is printed once after the container exits.
Defaults to True.

title: The dynalogging.py module > Description
text:
Logging module supplying a general framework for logging in the PyDYNA
pre service.
This module is built upon the Python logging
module. It is not intended to replace this Python module but rather to provide a way
for it and the PyDyna pre service to interact.
The loggers used in this PyDyna logging mdoule include the name of the instance,
which is intended to be unique. This name is printed in all the active
outputs and is used to track the different instances of the PyDyNA pre
service.
Description
pre
pre
pre

title: The dynalogging.py module > Global logger
text:
The global logger, named pydyna_global, is created at
ansys.dyna.core.__init__.  If you want to use the global logger,
you must call it at the top of your module:
You can rename the global logger to avoid conflicts with other loggers (if any):
The default logging level of LOG is ERROR. To change this to output
lower-level messages, you can use this code:
Alternatively, you can set the logging level of LOG to DEBUG
with one line of code:
LOG.setLevel(“DEBUG”)
Using the preceding line ensures that all the handlers are set to the input log level.
By default, this logger does not log to a file. If you want to log to a file,
you can add a file handler:
The preceding code sets the logger to also redirect to a file. If you want
to change the characteristics of the global logger from the beginning
of the execution, you must edit the __init__ file in the
ansys.dyna.core.pre directory.
To log using this logger, call the desired method as a normal logger.

title: The dynalogging.py module > Instance loggers
text:
Every time an instance of the Mapdl
class is created, a logger is created and stored in two places:
_MapdlCore._log: For backward compatibility.
LOG._instances: This field is a dictionary  where the key is the name
of the created logger.
Instance loggers inheritate the pydyna_global output handlers and
logging level unless otherwise specified. Instance loggers work in a
similar way to the global logger. You can use the
log_to_file() method to add
a file handler or the logger.Logging.setLevel() method to change
the log level.

title: Switch > Switch > Switch
text:
class ansys.dyna.core.pre.dynabase.Switch(*args, **kwds)

Bases: enum.Enum

Create a collection of name/value pairs.

Example enumeration:

>>> class Color(Enum):
...     RED = 1
...     BLUE = 2
...     GREEN = 3

Access them by:

attribute access:

>>> Color.RED
<Color.RED: 1>

value lookup:

>>> Color(1)
<Color.RED: 1>

name lookup:

>>> Color['RED']
<Color.RED: 1>

Enumerations can be iterated over, and know how many members they have:

>>> len(Color)
3

>>> list(Color)
[<Color.RED: 1>, <Color.BLUE: 2>, <Color.GREEN: 3>]

Methods can be added to enumerations, and members can have their own
attributes – see the documentation for details.

!! processed by numpydoc !!

title: DynaEM > Method detail
text:
Enable the EM solver and set its options.
EM solver. The default is 0. Options are:
EQ.-1: Turn off the EM solver after reading the EM keywords.
EQ.1: Eddy current solver.
EQ.2: Induced heating solver.
EQ.3: Resistive heating solver.
EQ.11: Electrophysiology monodomain.
EQ.12: Electrophysiology bidomain.
EQ.13: Electrophysiology monodomain coupled with bidomain.
Number of local EM steps in a whole period for when emsol = 2.
The default is 100.
Macro time step for when emsol = 2. The default is 0.
Number of electromagnetism cycles between the recalculation of FEM matrices.
The default is 5000.
Number of electromagnetism cycles between the recalculation of BEM matrices.
The default is 5000.
True when successful, False when failed.
Control the EM time step and its evolution.
Time step type. Options are:
EQ.1: Constant time step given in DTCONST
EQ.2: Time step as a function of time given by a load curve specified in LCID
EQ.3: Automatic time step computation, depending on the solver type
This time step is then multiplied by FACTOR.
Constant value for the time step for when tstype = 1.
True when successful, False when failed.
Create an optional card for defining options on electromagnetic contacts between two sets of parts.
Electromagnetic contact ID. The default is 0.
Detection type.  The default is 0. Options are:
EQ.0: Contact type 0
EQ.1: Contact type 1
Master part set ID. The default is 0.
Slave part set ID.  The default is 0.
First contact coefficient for contact detection conditions.
The default is 0.3.
Second contact coefficient for contact detection conditions.
The default is 0.3.
Third contact coefficient for contact detection conditions.
The default is 0.3.
Contact condition 3 when dtype = 1.
True when successful, False when failed.
Define Rogowsky coils to measure a global current versus time through a segment set or a node set.
Rogowsky coil ID.
Segment or node set ID.
Type of set. The default is 1. Options are:
EQ.1: Segment set
EQ.2: Node set
Type of current measured. The default is 1. Options are:
EQ.1: Volume current
EQ.2: Surface current (not available yet}
EQ.3: Magnetic field flow (B field times area)
True when successful, False when failed.
Create an electromagnetic material type and set properties
for a material whose permeability equals the free space permeability.
Material ID.
Electromagnetism type of the material. Options are:
EQ.0: Air or vacuum
EQ.1: Insulator material (These materials have the same electromagnetism behavior as EQ.0.)
EQ.2: Conductor carrying a source
EQ.3: Fluid conductor
EQ.4: Conductor not connected to any current or voltage source, where the eddy current problem is solved
Initial electrical conductivity of the material.
True when successful, False when failed.
Create an electromagnetic material type and set properties
whose permeability is different than the free space permeability.
Material ID.
Electromagnetism type of the material. Options are:
EQ.0: Air or vacuum
EQ.1: Insulator material (These materials have the same electromagnetism behavior as EQ.0.)
EQ.2: Conductor carrying a source
EQ.4: Conductor not connected to any current or voltage source, where the wddy current problem is solved
Initial electrical conductivity of the material.
ID of the EOS to use for the electrical conductivity.
Relative permeability, which is the ratio of the permeability of a specific
medium to the permeability of the free space.
True when successful, False when failed.
Turn on the monolithic FEM-BEM solver.
Monolithic solver type. The default is 0. The only option is EQ.0: Direct symmetric solver.
Solver type. The default is 0. Options are:
EQ.0: MINRES iterative solver
EQ.1: GMRES iterative solver
Absolute tolerance. The default is 1e-6.
Relative tolerance. The default is 1e-4.
Maximum number of iterations. The default is 500.
True when successful, False when failed.
Define the level of EM-related output on the screen and in the message file.
Level of matrix assembly output to show on the screen. The default is 0.
Options are:
EQ.0: No output
EQ.1: Basic assembly steps
EQ.2: Basic assembly steps + percentage completed + final statistics
EQ.3: Basic assembly steps + percentage completed + statistics at each percentage of completion
Level of matrix assembly output to write to the message file. The default
is 0. Options are:
EQ.0: No output
EQ.1: Basic assembly steps
EQ.2: Basic assembly steps + percentage completed + final statistics
EQ.3: Vasic assembly steps + percentage completed + statistics at each percentage of completion
Level of solver output to show on the screen. The default is 0. Options are:
EQ.0: No output
EQ.1: Global information at each FEM iteration
EQ.2: Detailed information at each FEM iteration

EQ.2: Detailed information at each FEM iteration
True when successful, False when failed.

Value of the resistance, voltage, or current depending on the isopotential
connection type. The default is 0.
Connection ID.
Enable the output of global EM.
Flag for whether to generate the output file. The default is 0.
Options are:

EQ.1: The output file is generated.
True when successful, False when failed.

ID of the magnet.
Part ID.
Magnet definition type. Options are:

EQ.4: Magnet defined by a global vector orientation given by two node IDs

title: DynaEM > Method detail > create_em_control
text:
DynaEM.create_em_control(emsol=0, numls=100, macrodt=0, ncylfem=5000, ncylbem=5000)

Enable the EM solver and set its options.

Parameters

emsol

python:int, optional

EM solver. The default is 0. Options are:

EQ.-1: Turn off the EM solver after reading the EM keywords.

EQ.1: Eddy current solver.

EQ.2: Induced heating solver.

EQ.3: Resistive heating solver.

EQ.11: Electrophysiology monodomain.

EQ.12: Electrophysiology bidomain.

EQ.13: Electrophysiology monodomain coupled with bidomain.

numls

python:int, optional

Number of local EM steps in a whole period for when emsol = 2.
The default is 100.

macrodt

python:int, optional

Macro time step for when emsol = 2. The default is 0.

ncylfem

python:int, optional

Number of electromagnetism cycles between the recalculation of FEM matrices.
The default is 5000.

ncylbem

python:int, optional

Number of electromagnetism cycles between the recalculation of BEM matrices.
The default is 5000.

Returns

bool

True when successful, False when failed.

!! processed by numpydoc !!

title: DynaEM > Method detail > create_em_timestep
text:
DynaEM.create_em_timestep(tstype, dtconst)

Control the EM time step and its evolution.

Parameters

tstype

python:int

Time step type. Options are:

EQ.1: Constant time step given in DTCONST

EQ.2: Time step as a function of time given by a load curve specified in LCID

EQ.3: Automatic time step computation, depending on the solver type

This time step is then multiplied by FACTOR.

dtconst

python:float

Constant value for the time step for when tstype = 1.

Returns

bool

True when successful, False when failed.

Define the level of EM-related output on the screen and in the message file.

Parameters

mats

python:int, optional

Level of matrix assembly output to show on the screen. The default is 0.
Options are:

EQ.0: No output

EQ.1: Basic assembly steps

EQ.2: Basic assembly steps + percentage completed + final statistics

EQ.3: Basic assembly steps + percentage completed + statistics at each percentage of completion

python:int, optional

Level of matrix assembly output to write to the message file. The default
is 0. Options are:

EQ.0: No output

EQ.1: Basic assembly steps

EQ.2: Basic assembly steps + percentage completed + final statistics

EQ.3: Vasic assembly steps + percentage completed + statistics at each percentage of completion

sols

python:int

Level of solver output to show on the screen. The default is 0. Options are:

EQ.0: No output

EQ.1: Global information at each FEM iteration

EQ.2: Detailed information at each FEM iteration

solf

python:int, optional

Level of solver output to write to the message file. The default is 0.
Options are:

EQ.0: No output

EQ.1: Global information at each FEM iteration

EQ.2: Detailed information at each FEM iteration

bool

True when successful, False when failed.

title: The dynalogging.py module > Description
text:
Logging module supplying a general framework for logging in the PyDYNA
pre service.
This module is built upon the Python logging
module. It is not intended to replace this Python module but rather to provide a way
for it and the PyDyna pre service to interact.
The loggers used in this PyDyna logging mdoule include the name of the instance,
which is intended to be unique. This name is printed in all the active
outputs and is used to track the different instances of the PyDyNA pre
service.
Description
pre
pre
pre

title: The dynalogging.py module > Global logger
text:
The global logger, named pydyna_global, is created at
ansys.dyna.core.__init__.  If you want to use the global logger,
you must call it at the top of your module:
You can rename the global logger to avoid conflicts with other loggers (if any):
The default logging level of LOG is ERROR. To change this to output
lower-level messages, you can use this code:
Alternatively, you can set the logging level of LOG to DEBUG
with one line of code:
LOG.setLevel(“DEBUG”)
Using the preceding line ensures that all the handlers are set to the input log level.
By default, this logger does not log to a file. If you want to log to a file,
you can add a file handler:
The preceding code sets the logger to also redirect to a file. If you want
to change the characteristics of the global logger from the beginning
of the execution, you must edit the __init__ file in the
ansys.dyna.core.pre directory.
To log using this logger, call the desired method as a normal logger.

title: The dynalogging.py module > Instance loggers
text:
Every time an instance of the Mapdl
class is created, a logger is created and stored in two places:
_MapdlCore._log: For backward compatibility.
LOG._instances: This field is a dictionary  where the key is the name
of the created logger.
Instance loggers inheritate the pydyna_global output handlers and
logging level unless otherwise specified. Instance loggers work in a
similar way to the global logger. You can use the
log_to_file() method to add
a file handler or the logger.Logging.setLevel() method to change
the log level.

Implicit model

title: Implicit model > Start the pre service
text:
Before starting the pre service, you must ensure that the Docker container
for this service has been started. For more information, see “Start the Docker
container for the pre service” in https://dyna.docs.pyansys.com/version/stable/index.html.
The pre service can also be started locally, please download the latest version of
ansys-pydyna-pre-server.zip package from https://github.com/ansys/pydyna/releases and start it
referring to the README.rst file in this server package.
Once the pre service is running, you can connect a client to it using
the hostname and port. This example uses the default localhost and port
("localhost" and "50051" respectively).
Start the pre service

title: Implicit model > Import initial mesh data
text:
Import the initial mesh data (nodes and elements), which includes the
vehicle data, weld data, and platen data.
Import initial mesh data

title: Implicit model > Define global control cards
text:
Because roof crush is a quasi-static loading case, you must run this model
as an implicit dynamic solution. Define the global control cards. From
the dynasolution class, set the termination time and the frequency for
the database ASCII options.
Use the implicit analysis methods in the dynamech class to define
the IMPLICIT control cards.
Set the automatic timestep control flag
and the optimal equilibrium iteration count per timestep.
Use the set_dynamic() method to set the IMASS value to 1 and assign the
gamma and beta values.
If normal modes must be extracted, use the set_eigenvalue() method.
The set_solution() method defines NSOLVR as 12 (Nolinear with BFGS update).
Define global control cards
dynasolution
dynamech
set_dynamic()
set_eigenvalue()
set_solution()

title: Implicit model > Define materials
text:
This model uses four classes of material: MAT_NULL, MAT_RIGID,
MAT_SPOTWELD, and MAT_PIECEWISE_LINEAR_PLASTICITY. Use the dynamaterial
class to define these materials.
Define materials
MAT_NULL
MAT_RIGID,
MAT_SPOTWELD
MAT_PIECEWISE_LINEAR_PLASTICITY
dynamaterial

title: Implicit model > Assign section and material properties
text:
Once all materials are explicitly defined, these material IDs must be cross-referenced
in the PART card. You use the set_material() method for this. Because many parts
share common materials, the assignment happens within a loop. Within the loop, the
set_element_formulation() method is called to assign the elform for the beam and
shell elements. Accordingly, either the beam diameter or the shell thickness is also
defined. To identify the part ID that has a particular material type, a predefined
list is made available in the camry_rc_data.py file, which this script reads.
Assign section and material properties
set_material()
set_element_formulation()
camry_rc_data.py

title: Implicit model > Generate keywords for spotwelds and nodal rigid bodies
section: Generate keywords for spotwelds and nodal rigid bodies
text:
The camry_rc_data.py file contains the predefined node pairs and node sets
required for the CONSTRAINED_SPOTWELD and CONSTRAINED_NODAL_RIGID_BODY
definitions. Loop through these lists to generate the appropriate keywords.
Generate keywords for spotwelds and nodal rigid bodies
camry_rc_data.py

title: Implicit model > Define contacts
text:
There are three contacts defined in this model:
Automatic single surface contact for the BIW self contact
Surface-to-surface contact between the platen and the BIW self contact
Tied contact for the spotweld beams
Use the ContactSurface() method to set the SSTYPE and MSTYPE.
The PartSet() method accepts the name of a list and converts it to
PART_SET_LIST. Notice how the contact type and category can be used
to create the three different type of contacts for this model.
Define contacts
ContactSurface()
PartSet()

title: Implicit model > Define SPCs
text:
You can use the boundaryconditions class to define both SPCs and
prescribed motions. The bottom of the BIW is SPCed by selecting a few nodes.
The prescribed motion is then assigned to the platen.
Define SPCs

text:
To use the solver features of PyDYNA, you must have a valid LS-DYNA license.
For information on getting a licensed copy of LS-DYNA, see
the Ansys LS-DYNA
page on the Ansys website.
Install PyDYNA and learn about the different installation modes.
Learn about the different modules in PyDYNA.
Run a working example of different modules in PyDYNA.

>>> class Color(Enum):
...     RED = 1

<Color.RED: 1>

Enumerations can be iterated over, and know how many members they have:

>>> len(Color)
3

[<Color.RED: 1>, <Color.BLUE: 2>, <Color.GREEN: 3>]

title: PymapdlCustomAdapter
text:
Bases: logging.LoggerAdapter
Adapter for keeping the reference to an MAPDL instance name dynamic.
Using the standard approach, extra parameters must be supplied
to the logger to indicate the MAPDL instance for which messages
must be logged.
With this class, you only have to specify the MAPDL instance
that you are referring to once.
PymapdlCustomAdapter
PymapdlCustomAdapter
logging.LoggerAdapter

text:
class ansys.dyna.core.pre.dynalogging.PymapdlCustomAdapter(logger, extra=None)

Bases: logging.LoggerAdapter

Adapter for keeping the reference to an MAPDL instance name dynamic.

Using the standard approach, extra parameters must be supplied
to the logger to indicate the MAPDL instance for which messages
must be logged.

With this class, you only have to specify the MAPDL instance
that you are referring to once.

Process the logging message and keyword arguments passed in to
a logging call to insert contextual information. You can either
manipulate the message itself, the keyword args or both. Return
the message and kwargs modified (or not) to suit your needs.
Normally, you’ll only need to override this one method in a
LoggerAdapter subclass for your specific needs.
Add a file handler to the logger.
Name of the file where logs are recorded. The default is FILE_NAME.
Level of logging. The default is LOG_LEVEL, which causes all messages
to be recorded. For example, you can set the level of logging to DEBUG.

Level of logging record. The default is LOG_LEVEL, which causes all messages
to be recorded. For example, you can set the level of logging to "DEBUG".

title: Logger > Logger > Logger
text:
class ansys.dyna.core.solver.dynalogging.Logger(level=logging.DEBUG, to_file=False, to_stdout=True, filename=FILE_NAME)

Provides the logger used for each PyDyna pre session.

This class allows you to add handlers to the logger to output to a file or
standard output.

Parameters

level

python:int, optional

Logging level to filter the message severity allowed in the logger.
The default is logging.DEBUG.

to_file

bool, optional

Whether to write log messages to a file. The default is False.

to_stdout

bool, optional

Whether to write log messages to the standard output. The default is
True.

filename

python:str, optional

Name of the file to write log messages to if to_file=True. The default
is FILE_NAME.

Examples

Demonstrate logger usage from an MAPDL instance mapdl. This logger is
automatically created when an MAPDL instance is created.

>>> from ansys.mapdl.core import launch_mapdl
>>> mapdl = launch_mapdl(loglevel='DEBUG')
>>> mapdl._log.info('This is a useful message')
INFO -  -  <ipython-input-24-80df150fe31f> - <module> - This is LOG debug message.

Import the global PYMAPDL logger and add a file output handler.

>>> import os
>>> from ansys.mapdl.core import LOG
>>> file_path = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), 'pymapdl.log')
>>> LOG.log_to_file(file_path)

!! processed by numpydoc !!

title: Logger > Method detail > log_to_file
text:
Logger.log_to_file(filename=FILE_NAME, level=LOG_LEVEL)

Add a file handler to logger.

Parameters

filename

python:str, optional

Name of the file where the logs are recorded. The default
is FILE_NAME, in which case they are recorded in the
'pymapdl.log' file.

level

python:str, optional

Level of logging. The default is LOG_LEVEL, in which
case 'DEBUG' is used.

Examples

Write to the pymapdl.log file in the current working directory.

>>> from ansys.mapdl.core import LOG
>>> import os
>>> file_path = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), 'pymapdl.log')
>>> LOG.log_to_file(file_path)

title: Logger > Method detail > add_child_logger
text:
Logger.add_child_logger(suffix, level=None)

Add a child logger to the main logger.

This child logger is more general than an instance logger, which is designed to
track the state of MAPDL instances.

If the logging level is in the arguments, a new logger with a reference
to the _global logger handlers is created instead of a child logger.

Parameters

suffix

python:str

Name of the logger.

level

python:str, optional

Level of logging. The default is None.

Returns

logging.logger

title: Logger > Method detail > add_instance_logger
text:
Logger.add_instance_logger(name, mapdl_instance, level=None)

Create a logger for an MAPDL instance.

The MAPDL instance logger is a logger with an adapter that adds
contextual information, such as the MAPDL instance name. This logger is
returned, and you can use it to log events as a normal logger. It is also
stored in the _instances field.

Parameters

name

python:str

Name for the new logger.

mapdl_instance

ansys.mapdl.core.mapdl._MapdlCore

MAPDL instance object. This should contain the name attribute.

Returns

ansys.mapdl.core.logging.PymapdlCustomAdapter

Logger adapter customized to add MAPDL information to the
logs.  You can use this class to log events in the same
way you use the logger class.

Raises

Exception

You can only input strings as name to this method.

An example of a sweep over impact velocities for this problem can be found in this repository at

title: Getting started > Keyword and run example > Preprocessing
text:
The following code describes an LS-DYNA Model for a Taylor bar impact problem. It assumes that the mesh file
taylor_bar_mesh.k exists in the working directory. This mesh file can be found in this repository at
examples/Taylor_Bar/taylor_bar_mesh.k.
Preprocessing
examples/Taylor_Bar/taylor_bar_mesh.k

title: Getting started > Keyword and run example > Preview
text:
The following code opens a 3D graphics window to preview the mesh for the LS-DYNA Model
Preview

title: Getting started > Keyword and run example > Write to file
text:
The following code writes the LS-DYNA model to an input.k keyword file in the working directory.
Write to file

title: Getting started > Keyword and run example > Solve
text:
The following code runs LS-DYNA using the input.k file.
Solve

title: Getting started > Keyword and run example > Post processing
text:
The following code processes results and generates a line chart of Time vs. Energy from the impact. This requires an installation
of a matplotlib backend.
Post processing
matplotlib

title: Getting started > Keyword and run example > Pre and solver example
text:
The next few sections show how to preprocessing, solve, and postprocessing a ball plate example.
Pre and solver example

title: Getting started > Keyword and run example > Preprocessing
text:
The following code processes a ball plate example. In the repository, you can get the
input file from src/ansys/dyna/core/pre/examples/explicit/ball_plate/ball_plate.k and
the Python file from examples/Explicit/ball_plate.py.

>>> class Color(Enum):
...     RED = 1
...     BLUE = 2
...     GREEN = 3

Access them by:

attribute access:

value lookup:

<Color.RED: 1>

name lookup:

Enumerations can be iterated over, and know how many members they have:

>>> len(Color)
3

>>> list(Color)
[<Color.RED: 1>, <Color.BLUE: 2>, <Color.GREEN: 3>]

Methods can be added to enumerations, and members can have their own
attributes – see the documentation for details.

title: Getting started > Run > Use PyDYNA to run LSDYNA locally
section: Use PyDYNA to run LSDYNA locally
text:
Run DYNA using ansys.dyna.core.run on a local machine,
this does not require Docker.
Use PyDYNA to run LSDYNA locally
ansys.dyna.core.run

title: Getting started > Run > Run LS-DYNA using ansys.dyna.core.run
text: Run LS-DYNA using ansys.dyna.core.run

title: Getting started > Run > How it works
text:
run_dyna attempts to find an installation of the LS-DYNA solver on your machine.
It uses the Python dependency ansys-tools-path to discover where LS-DYNA is installed.
After installing ansys-tools-path, the location of LS-DYNA can be saved by running
save-ansys-path --name dyna {path/to/dyna} so that subsequent usages of run_dyna
look there.
How it works
run_dyna
ansys-tools-path
ansys-tools-path
save-ansys-path --name dyna {path/to/dyna}
run_dyna

Bases: ansys.dyna.core.pre.dynabase.Enum

Create a collection of name/value pairs.

Example enumeration:

>>> class Color(Enum):
...     RED = 1
...     BLUE = 2
...     GREEN = 3

Access them by:

attribute access:

>>> Color.RED
<Color.RED: 1>

value lookup:

>>> Color(1)
<Color.RED: 1>

name lookup:

>>> Color['RED']
<Color.RED: 1>

Enumerations can be iterated over, and know how many members they have:

>>> len(Color)
3

>>> list(Color)
[<Color.RED: 1>, <Color.BLUE: 2>, <Color.GREEN: 3>]

Methods can be added to enumerations, and members can have their own
attributes – see the documentation for details.

class ansys.dyna.core.pre.dynamech.Airbag(set, heat_capacity_at_constant_volume=0, heat_capacity_at_constant_pressure=0, input_gas_temperature=0, input_mass_flow_rate=Curve(x=[], y=[]), shape_factor_for_exit_hole=0, ambient_pressure=0, ambient_density=0)

Bases: ansys.dyna.core.pre.dynabase.BaseObj

Defines an airbag or control volume.

Parameters

Set

SegmentSet or PartSet

Set. Options are:

EQ.0: Segment set ID.

EQ.1: Part set ID.

heat_capacity_at_constant_volume

python:float, optional

Heat capacity at constant volume. The default is 0.

heat_capacity_at_constant_pressure

python:float, optional

Heat capacity at constant pressure. The default is 0.

input_gas_temperature

python:float, optional

Temperature of the input gas. The default is 0.

input_mass_flow_rate

python:int, optional

Load curve ID specifying the input mass flow rate.
The default is 0.

shape_factor_for_exit_hole

python:float, optional

Shape factor for exit hole. The default is 0.

ambient_pressure

python:float, optional

Ambient pressure. The default is 0.

ambient_density

python:float, optional

Ambient density. The default is 0.

Returns

bool

True when successful, False when failed..

dpf.connect_to_server()

title: Airbag deploy postprocessing > Load model
text:
Load the model and print the contents of the model. All parts in the model
are shell parts. Model information includes the result components and the
number of states available in the d3plot.
Load model

title: Airbag deploy postprocessing > Extract stress on all parts
text:
Extract the stress on all the parts. The stress fields container is scoped to
all time frequencies to be able to animate the change in stress on the
airbag fabric.

points by default. Extract the stress on the mid integration point.

title: Thermal stress example
text:
This example shows how to create a thermal stress model with the PyDYNA pre service.
The executable file for LS-DYNA is ls-dyna_smp_s_R13.0_365-gf8a97bda2a_winx64_ifort190.exe.
Thermal stress example
pre
ls-dyna_smp_s_R13.0_365-gf8a97bda2a_winx64_ifort190.exe

title: Thermal stress example > Start the pre service
text:
Before starting the pre service, you must ensure that the Docker container
for this service has been started. For more information, see “Start the Docker
container for the pre service” in https://dyna.docs.pyansys.com/version/stable/index.html.
The pre service can also be started locally, please download the latest version of
ansys-pydyna-pre-server.zip package from https://github.com/ansys/pydyna/releases and start it
refefring to the README.rst file in this server package.
Once the pre servic is running, you can connect a client to it using
the hostname and the port. This example uses the default local host and port
("localhost" and "50051" respectively).
Start the pre service
pre

thermal_stress.k

title: EMType > EMType > EMType
text:
class ansys.dyna.core.pre.dynaem.EMType(*args, **kwds)

Bases: ansys.dyna.core.pre.dynabase.Enum

Create a collection of name/value pairs.

Example enumeration:

>>> class Color(Enum):
...     RED = 1
...     BLUE = 2
...     GREEN = 3

Access them by:

attribute access:

>>> Color.RED
<Color.RED: 1>

value lookup:

>>> Color(1)
<Color.RED: 1>

name lookup:

>>> Color['RED']
<Color.RED: 1>

Enumerations can be iterated over, and know how many members they have:

>>> len(Color)
3

>>> list(Color)
[<Color.RED: 1>, <Color.BLUE: 2>, <Color.GREEN: 3>]

Methods can be added to enumerations, and members can have their own
attributes – see the documentation for details.

Determine if a port is in use at a given host.

port

python:int

Port.

host

Host. The default is LOCALHOST, in which case "127.0.0.1"

is used.

Returns

True when a port is in use at the given host, False otherwise.



Notes

The port must “bind” the address. Just checking if a socket can be created
is insufficient because it is possible to run into permission
errors like this one:

launcher.launch_remote_dynapre(version=None, cleanup_on_exit=True) -> ansys.dyna.core.pre.dynasolution.DynaSolution

Start DYNA PRE remotely using the product instance management API.

When calling this method, you need to ensure that you are in an environment where PyPIM is configured.
This can be verified with pypim.is_configured.

Parameters

version

python:str, optional

The DYNA version to run, in the 3 digits format, such as “212”.

If unspecified, the version will be chosen by the server.

cleanup_on_exit

bool, optional

Exit DYNA when python exits or the dyna Python instance is
garbage collected.

If unspecified, it will be cleaned up.

title: Ball plate
text:
This example shows how to use the PyDYNA pre service to create
a ball plate model. The executable file for LS-DYNA is
ls-dyna_smp_d_R13.0_365-gf8a97bda2a_winx64_ifort190.exe.
Ball plate
pre
ls-dyna_smp_d_R13.0_365-gf8a97bda2a_winx64_ifort190.exe

title: Ball plate > Perform required imports
text:
Perform the required imports.
Perform required imports

title: Ball plate > Start the pre service
text:
Before starting the pre service, you must ensure that the Docker container
for this service has been started. For more information, see “Start the Docker
container for the pre service” in https://dyna.docs.pyansys.com/version/stable/index.html.
The pre service can also be started locally, please download the latest version of
ansys-pydyna-pre-server.zip package from https://github.com/ansys/pydyna/releases and start it
refefring to the README.rst file in this server package.
Once the pre service is running, you can connect a client to it using
the host name and port. This code uses the default localhost and port
("localhost" and "50051" respectively).
Start the pre service
pre
pre
pre
pre
pre
"localhost"
"50051"

title: Ball plate > Start the solution workflow
text:
NODES and ELEMENTS are read in from the ball_plate.k file. This file
also has the PART defined in it, but the section and material fields are
empty to begin with.
Start the solution workflow
ball_plate.k

For the D3plots, set simulation termination time, simulation timestep, and
output frequency.

The dynamaterials class is used to define these materials: MAT_RIGID,

section: Define section properties and assign materials

Now that you have materials with the material ID corresponding to
the Part ID, you can assign these materials to the
parts. You can also define section properties, element
formulations, and constraints.

Constrain the nodes in a list of single point constraints (spc).

Use the create_velocity_node method
to initialize the velocity components in the desired direction.

title: Getting started > Modules
text:
PyDYNA consists of four modules, ansys.dyna.core.pre, ansys.dyna.core.solver,
ansys.dyna.core.run, and ansys.dyna.core.keywords.
learn about the different modules in PyDYNA.
Interact with LS-DYNA keywords using the keywords module.
Run LS-DYNA using the run module.
Run the LS-DYNA server locally or in a Docker container.
